title: Trial Specifications Document
text:
1 Introduction
This Trial Specifications Document (TSD) describes the design and technical details of the Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) of South Atlantic albacore tuna (Thunnus alalunga; SALB), including the operating models, candidate management procedures, and performance metrics used to evaluate them.
The TSD is a living document that will be updated as the MSE progresses to reflect the current state of the analysis.
For more information on the South Atlantic Albacore (SALB) MSE process see the project homepage.

title: Trial Specifications Document
text:
1.1 SALB MSE Framework
The SALB MSE is built using the openMSE framework. The analysis is fully reproducible: all code, functions, and objects used to build the operating models, candidate management procedures, and performance indicators are available in associated GitHub repository.
To reproduce the analysis, download the GitHub repository, and work through the scripts in the analysis directory.
The operating models (OMs) were conditioned with Stock Synthesis 3 (SS3; (Methot and Wetzel 2013)) and were based on the 2026 Albacore Stock Assessment (Chapter 2).




Methot, Richard D., and Chantell R. Wetzel. 2013. “Stock Synthesis: A Biological and Statistical Framework for Fish Stock Assessment and Fishery Management.” Fisheries Research 142 (May): 86–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fishres.2012.10.012.

title: 2  Stock Assessment
text:
2.1 Fleet Structure and Fishery Data
The first age-structured stock assessment for South Atlantic Albacore was completed in June 2026, using the Stock Synthesis 3 (SS3) framework (Methot and Wetzel 2013). The assessment output was used as the basis for the Reference (Chapter 3) and Robustness (Chapter 4) operating models.
This chapter provides a brief overview of the assessment. For full details, see the Report of the 2026 ICCAT Albacore Stock Assessment Meeting and the Report of the 2026 ICCAT Albacore Data Preparatory Meeting.
The assessment model integrated total catches, standardized indices of abundance, and length-composition data covering the period 1956 – 2024.
Five fishing fleets were defined by aggregating catch and length composition data for fishing fleets with similar properties (Table 2.1; Figure 2.1; Figure 2.2).
Five standardized CPUE indices were used in the Base Case assessment (Table 2.2; Figure 2.1; Figure 2.3).
The catch and CPUE data are imported into the Reference (Chapter 3) and Robustness (Chapter 4) operating models, and made available to the candidate management procedures (Chapter 7).
Figure 2.1: Overview of the data used in the 2026 stock assessment.
Figure 2.2: Time series plot of the total catch data used in the 2026 assessment.
Figure 2.3: Time series plot of the five CPUE indices used in the Base Case 2026 assessment.

title: 2  Stock Assessment
text:
2.2 Biological Parameters
The assessment model assumed age-specific natural mortality modelled with the Lorezen function (Lorenzen et al. 2022), mean length-at-age described by the von Bertalanffy growth model, and the stock-recruitment relationship modelled with the Beverton-Holt equation, parameterized with the ‘steepness’ (h) formulation.
Recruitment variability \(\left(\sigma_R\right)\) was fixed at 0.4 and h at 0.75. Maturity was specified as an at-age schedule with fish reaching 50% maturity at age 5 years and full maturity at 6 years.
See Table 5 in the Data Preparatory Report for a full list of the life-history parameters used in the model.
Uncertainty in the biological parameters was focused on alternative mean length-at-age and naturality mortality-at-age schedules. A set of nine models were developed combining three alternative growth parameterizations and three alternative natural mortality estimates corresponding to the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of the distributions agreed at the Data Preparatory Meeting.
These nine models are used to create the Reference OM Grid (Chapter 3). The central model of this grid (model 5, referred to as S05) was used considered the Base Case assessment model and is used in the MSE as the Reference OM.




Lorenzen, Kai, Edward V. Camp, and Taryn M. Garlock. 2022. “Natural Mortality and Body Size in Fish Populations.” Fisheries Research 252 (August): 106327. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fishres.2022.106327.


Methot, Richard D., and Chantell R. Wetzel. 2013. “Stock Synthesis: A Biological and Statistical Framework for Fish Stock Assessment and Fishery Management.” Fisheries Research 142 (May): 86–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fishres.2012.10.012.

title: 3  Reference Operating Models
text:
3.1 Overview of Population Dynamics
The Reference Set OMs are currently designed as a factorial uncertainty grid with the two axes of uncertainty (Growth & Natural Mortality) developed during the 2026 Stock Assessment (see Chapter 2).
The Growth and Natural Mortality axes were both represented with three levels, corresponding to the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of the estimated parameter distributions. The 50th percentile level for each axis matches the Base Case stock assessment model (S05).
The OM imported from this model is referred to as the Reference OM.
The three levels for each axis produces a 3 x 3 uncertainty grid with 9 reference operating models (Table 3.1). The von Bertalanffy growth parameters and the reference Lorenzen M value (at reference age 11) at each percentile level are shown in Table 3.2.
Figure 3.1 shows the length- and natural mortality-at-age curves for each of the 9 reference grid OMs.
Each operating model was imported from the maximum likelihood estimates (MLE) of the corresponding SS3 run, so within each OM the historical population dynamics are identical across simulations.
The figures below provide an overview of the population dynamics described in the nine Reference OMs:
Table 3.3 summarises the MSY reference points, along with terminal year \(F/F_\text{MSY}\) and \(SB/SB_\text{MSY}\), for each of the nine Reference OMs.
Figure 3.2: Historical spawning biomass (\(SB\)), coloured by operating model and faceted by the axes of uncertainty
Figure 3.3: Historical spawning biomass depletion (\(SB/SB_0\)), by operating model.
Figure 3.4: Historical spawning biomass relative to MSY level (\(SB/SB_{\text{MSY}}\)), by operating model.
Figure 3.5: Historical fishing mortality relative to FMSY level (\(F/F_{\text{MSY}}\)), by operating model.

title: 4  Robustness Operating Models
text:
Note



Robustness OMs have not yet been developed for SALB.
Robustness OMs will be discussed by the SALB MSE Working Group and this chapter will be updated once those models have been defined.


At the 2026 Stock Assessment, the Group identified the following uncertainties that should be evaluated in the MSE:

alternative CPUE configurations
alternative biological hypotheses

Additional uncertainties that could be evaluated in robustness OMs include:

uncertainty in TAC implementation (e.g., overages in TAC)
impacts of climate change (e.g., time-varying life-history parameters)
alternative recruitment scenarios.

title: 5  Forward Projections
text:
5.1 Management Cycle and Data Lag
This chapter describes the assumptions and settings for the projection years.
Each operating model has 100 independent simulations.
The MSE assumed that the first TAC generated by the CMPs will be set in 2028, with a 3-year management cycle and and a 2-year data lag .
This means that the TAC that will be implemented for the 2028 fishing year will be calculated in 2027 using data up to 2025, and then from there the TAC is updated every 3 years (Table 5.1).

title: 5  Forward Projections
text:
5.2 Initial Projection Years
Following the stock assessment, the OMs have a historical period spanning from 1956 to 2024. Given that the TAC advice generated by the CMPs will be implemented in 2028, the first 3 years of the projection period (2025, 2026, 2027) are, in effect, an extension of the historical period rather than years managed by the CMPs.
Where real catch data (and, for the indices used by index-based CMPs, real CPUE data) become available for these years, they will be used directly in the OM. Where data are not yet available, fleet-specific catches for these years will instead be simulated by resampling from the mean and standard deviation of each fleet’s catches over the preceding four years.
This is necessary primarily because the population dynamics model needs a catch value for every year in order to project the population forward in time. Additionally, the CMPs require a complete data stream up to the data-lag year in order to calculate the first TAC.
As shown in Table 5.1, the 2028 TAC is calculated in 2027 using data up to 2025. Because the historical period ends in 2024, 2025 falls within the projection period and will not yet be observed at the time this MSE is conditioned. For now, the simulated 2025 catch and CPUE index values will be used to calculate the 2028 TAC; once real 2025 data become available, these can replace the simulated values without requiring any change to the underlying MSE framework.

title: 5  Forward Projections
text:
5.3 Recruitment Deviations
Annual recruitment deviations are generated as autocorrelated, bias-corrected log-normal deviates around the stock-recruit relationship. For a given simulation, the (log-scale) deviation in year \(y\) is calculated as:
\[
\varepsilon_y \sim \text{TruncNormal}(\mu, \sigma_R,\ \pm 2\sigma_R)
\]
\[
x_y = \rho\, x_{y-1} + \varepsilon_y \sqrt{1-\rho^2}
\]
\[
\text{RecDev}_y = \exp(x_y)
\]
where \(\sigma_R\) is the recruitment deviation standard deviation, \(\rho\) is the lag-1 autocorrelation, and \(\mu = -0.5\sigma_R^2(1-\rho)/\sqrt{1-\rho^2}\) is a bias-correction term so that \(E[\text{RecDev}_y] = 1\) on the natural scale. Deviations are truncated at \(\pm 2\sigma_R\) (on the log scale) to avoid unrealistic extremes.

title: 5  Forward Projections
text:
5.4 Observation Error for Simulated Data
The data used by the CMPs (fleet-specific catches and standardised CPUE indices) are simulated from the true OM-generated values with multiplicative observation error.
For catch, the observation error is conditioned on the historical fit between the OM’s simulated catch and the actual reported historical catch. In practice, the assessment model is fit to the catch data essentially exactly, so the catch data provided to the CMPs exactly matches the catches in the OM.
For the CPUE indices, the same historical-conditioning approach is used, but with a lag-1 autocorrelation additionally estimated from the residuals between the observed and simulated index. Index observation error therefore follows the same truncated, autocorrelated log-normal process described above for recruitment deviations, continuing on from the last estimated historical residual.
Table 5.2 shows the fitted SD and lag-1 autocorrelation for each index, together with the last historical year in which the index was produced; all five indices are currently assumed to scale proportionally with true abundance (i.e. no hyperstability/hyperdepletion).
These values are specific to the Reference OM shown here as an example. The conditioned SD and autocorrelation will vary across the full set of Reference and Robustness OMs, since each is conditioned on its own historical fit.



Table 5.2: Conditioned CPUE index observation error for the Reference OM (as an example): SD of the log-scale residuals, lag-1 autocorrelation, and the last historical year each index was produced.

Figure 5.1 shows example realisations of the index observation error multiplier for three simulations, spanning the historical and projection periods, again for the Reference OM only.
It illustrates that the autocorrelated observation error applied in the projections has the same statistical properties as estimated from the historical period, with no discontinuity at the start of the projection period. Historical indices that are no longer produced are excluded, since these will not be generated or used in the projection period.







Figure 5.1: Example CPUE index observation error multipliers for three simulations (Reference OM only), spanning the historical and projection periods. The dashed line marks the end of the historical period. Discontinued indices are excluded (see text).

title: 6  Performance Metrics
text:
Resolution 24-09 (ICCAT 2024) describes the initial operational management objectives for Southern Atlantic Albacore, organised into four categories: Stock Status, Safety, Yield, and Stability.
Each performance metric (PM) below is calculated over the full 30-year projection period by default, for a given CMP, and can be summarised at two levels: for an individual OM, or across a set of OMs (e.g. the Reference Set).
In the latter case, the simulations from every OM in the set are first combined into a single pooled MSE object and the summary statistic (e.g. a probability or a mean) is then calculated once over that combined set of simulations. This allows CMP performance to be summarised both by individual uncertainty axis (e.g. by growth or natural mortality level) and overall, across the full Reference OM set.
Stock Status. The stock should have a 60% or greater probability of occurring in the green quadrant of the Kobe matrix over a 30-year projection period: i.e. \(SB > SB_{MSY}\) and \(F < F_{MSY}\) simultaneously.
Safety. There should be no greater than 15% probability of the stock falling below \(B_{LIM}\) at any point during the 30-year projection period, using an interim \(B_{LIM}\) of 40% of \(SB_{MSY}\) (footnote 1 of the Resolution). So that higher values are better for every PM, this is expressed as the probability of the stock remaining at or above \(B_{LIM}\) throughout the projection period, with a target of at least 85%.
Yield. The objective is to maximise overall catch levels. As the Resolution does not specify a particular statistic, the mean annual catch is used.
Stability. Any change in total allowable catch (TAC) between management periods should be 20% or less, noting that asymmetric stability limits (e.g. different thresholds for TAC increases versus decreases) may also be evaluated. This is expressed as the probability that the change in TAC between successive management cycles is less than 20%; i.e. calculated between the TAC values actually set every 3 years.
Table 6.1 summarises these objectives, the corresponding performance metric used to evaluate CMP performance against each, and the associated target. T


Table 6.1: Summary of the Resolution 24-09 (ICCAT 2024) initial operational management objectives and the corresponding performance metrics used to evaluate CMP performance.









Objective
Performance Metric
Formula
Target



Stock Status
Probability of occurring in the Kobe green quadrant
\(\text{Prob}\left(SB/SB_{MSY} > 1 \text{ and } F/F_{MSY} < 1\right)\)
\(\geq 60\%\)


Safety
Probability of remaining at or above \(B_{LIM}\) (40% \(SB_{MSY}\)) throughout the projection
\(\text{Prob}\left(\min_y(SB_y/SB_{MSY}) \geq 0.4\right)\)
\(\geq 85\%\)


Yield
Mean annual catch
\(\text{mean}_y(C_y)\)
Maximise (ranked; no fixed target)


Stability
Probability that the change in TAC between management periods is \(< 20\%\)

\(\text{Prob}\left(\lvert (TAC_c - TAC_{c-1})/TAC_{c-1}\rvert < 0.2\right)\)
Ranked (no fixed probability target)












NoteProjection Period Used for PM Calculation



These PMs are currently calculated over all projection years, starting in 2028 when the CMPs are first used to set a TAC (Chapter 5). The SALB MSE Technical Team may wish to consider excluding some initial number of years from this period, to allow time for a CMP’s TAC advice to actually take effect on the population dynamics before performance is assessed.






ICCAT. 2024. Resolution by ICCAT on Development of Initial Operational Management Objectives for Southern Atlantic Albacore. Nos. 24-09. International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas. https://www.iccat.int/documents/recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-09-e.pdf.

title: 7  Candidate Management Procedures
text:
Within the MSE framework, each candidate MP is applied to the historical data simulated by each operating model and then used to set management advice at each management cycle throughout the projection period. The performance of each MP is then compared across the Reference and Robustness OMs (see Chapter 3 and Chapter 4) using the performance metrics described in Chapter 6.
The candidate MPs described in this chapter are preliminary and intended to illustrate the range of approaches available to the SALB MSE Technical Team, spanning simple constant catch rules through to methods that respond to trends in an abundance index or to a full stock assessment model.






NotePreliminary Candidate MPs



The MPs described below are preliminary and have been included to test the MSE framework. The final set of candidate MPs will be developed and agreed with the SALB MSE Technical Team, and this chapter will be updated accordingly.


Table 7.1 summarises the candidate MPs currently coded in the MSE framework.


Table 7.1: Candidate management procedures currently coded in the MSE framework, grouped by general approach.








MP
Type
Description



SP_FMSY
Model-based
Sets the TAC to the catch expected to achieve MSY, based on a surplus production stock assessment model fitted to the simulated catch and index data.


SP_75FMSY
Model-based
As SP_FMSY, but sets the TAC to a more precautionary 75% of the level expected to achieve MSY.


IRatio
Index-based
Adjusts the TAC up or down depending on whether recent values of the abundance index are higher or lower than a historical reference period.


ISlope
Index-based
Adjusts the TAC up or down depending on the recent trend (slope) in the abundance index.


MCC1
Stepped, index-based
Adjusts a baseline TAC up or down through a series of pre-agreed steps, depending on the ratio of the recent abundance index to a historical reference period.


MCC2
Stepped, index-based
As MCC1, but starting from a slightly higher baseline TAC.
